republic_day.intro: Italy's national day on 2 June celebrates the 1946 referendum in which Italians chose a republic over the monarchy.
republic_day.history: On 2–3 June 1946, in the first nationwide vote open to women, Italians voted to abolish the monarchy after Fascism and the Second World War. The House of Savoy went into exile and the Italian Republic was born, its constitution taking effect in 1948.
republic_day.traditions: A military parade marches along the Via dei Fori Imperiali in Rome past the Altare della Patria, where the President lays a wreath at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. The Frecce Tricolori paint the sky green, white and red.
funFacts: 1946 was the first time Italian women voted in a national ballot.
The republic won with about 54% of the vote.
Male heirs of the House of Savoy were barred from Italy until 2002.

liberation_day.intro: 25 April marks the 1945 liberation of Italy from Nazi occupation and the Fascist regime at the end of the Second World War.
liberation_day.history: On 25 April 1945 the National Liberation Committee called a general uprising as Allied forces and Italian partisans drove out the German army and the last of Mussolini’s republic. The date became the symbol of the Resistance and a national holiday in 1949.
liberation_day.traditions: Cities hold marches, concerts and ceremonies with the partisan association ANPI. Wreaths are laid at Resistance memorials, and the partisan anthem ‚Bella Ciao‘ is sung across the country.
funFacts: ‚Bella Ciao‘ has become a worldwide protest song.
Mussolini was captured and killed by partisans days after 25 April 1945.
The day honours the partigiani of the Resistance.
